intro:
The obedience of Christ is a systematic theological category distinguishing his active obedience (lifelong positive fulfillment of the law's demands) from his passive obedience (his suffering of the law's penalty for sin). The distinction, developed especially in Reformed federal theology, draws on Romans 5:12-21, where Paul contrasts 'the one trespass' of Adam with 'the one act of righteousness' of Christ, and 'the disobedience of the one man' with 'the obedience of the one man' (5:18-19). Christ's obedience is the ground of justification: because he perfectly obeyed the law as the last Adam, his righteousness is reckoned to those united to him by faith.

The active obedience encompasses Jesus's lifelong submission to the law — circumcised on the eighth day, presented at the temple, observing feasts, 'fulfilling all righteousness' at his baptism (Matt. 3:15), resisting every temptation, keeping every precept. The passive obedience encompasses his submission to suffering and death as the penalty due to sinners, not to himself: he 'became obedient to death — even death on a cross' (Phil. 2:8). Hebrews 5:8-9 states the paradox: 'Although he was a son, he learned obedience from what he suffered and, once made perfect, he became the source of eternal salvation for all who obey him.' The obedience of Christ accomplishes what humans could not — a perfect active righteousness credited to those united to him by faith, and a perfect passive submission that bore their penalty — making it central to the doctrine of imputed righteousness in both Lutheran and Reformed soteriology.
